posts.title: Advice for Apprentice Electricians
posts.heroBlurb: Mr. Neistat is right. Stop being late!
posts.bodyHtml:
to flesh out more, I need to frame this better to explain the value of wanting to do the best you can


pasted from my notes:


electrician apprentice blog post on tips and things i’ve learned to be useful and do a good job (carry sharpie pencil knife etc) anticipate what tools people need and hand them to them if applicable to the situation (don't go overboard) always stay busy, default to cleaning up the space or immediately ask for the next thing to do. Pay attention, ask a lot of questions (why we do things this way or that way) if something seems wrong ask about it, better safe than sorry. Avoid using the bathroom excessively.

BE ON TIME - TO BE ON TIME, ARRIVE EARLY, LEAVE EARLY https://youtu.be/Hm4mr7jzSpQ?si=QPiUzzp7Kip1U7wI THE NUMBER ONE REASON PEOPLE ARE FIRED AT THE COMPANY IS FOR BEING LATE

PAY ATTENTION - Pay critical attention to everything your journeyman does, ask them why they do things they way they do, often there are safety critical reasons. It your responsibility to learn accurately, make sure that you are understanding the tasks they demonstrate to you!

Good Youtube channels: When you do anything seriously you should consume as much information as possible on the subject https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lDzLRBZ7WM8 palmer is decent, there are better channels I should put in here.

posts.title: This Website
posts.bodyHtml:
This website is mostly HTML with a CSS stylesheet. I developed part of it before Codex released then came back to it and got it to a finished state using that. 


Also on my github: a simple static post editor tool. It simply reads the website folder and adjusts the posts and homepage without me having to edit the HTML every time. It handles images, albums, and videos as well.


I'd been thinking about making a website for quite some time, glad to finally have somewhere to put all my interests in one place!

posts.title: Toolbox Mount
posts.bodyHtml:
pasted directly from journal:
I will come back and put in images and properly document later but I want to get this up first.

I was given an old truck toolbox as a gift from my old electrician boss. (I organized our storage unit and there were 3 of them taking up space) but unfortunately the best one just barely fit my truck in width, and definitely did not fit in depth.

There's a lot of different solutions to this problem, and if I wanted to solve it cheaply I'd just use wood and some bar stock, or weld onto the box, but I hadn't done any personal projects through sendcutsend yet and figured this was as good a time as any.

I initially planned to clamp in front of and behind the box, but after measuring how far the lid swings back I realized I could clamp to the inside of my truck rail (there's a spot for racks to insert to the body) so I did that.

--

Detailing my thought process on this project for posterity

Requirements:

  • offset toolbox vertically
  • mount the toolbox securely without permanent modifications

Alternative solutions to this problem:

  • I don't actually need to do this, there's a smaller toolbox that fits my truck that I could swap this one for in my old boss's storage unit (which he let me keep the key for). I prefer this one for a few reasons (cleaner, bigger, matches truck color better).
  • If I was broke and needed the toolbox mounted I'd just do this with box tubing and a flat sheet that I hacksawed (or the unistrut I have lying around).
  • Welding on clamp points to the side.
  • Bolting on clamp points (L channel)

--

Regardless I left it kicking around in my head for a few days until I'd figured out the design I wanted.

After I came up with a preliminary design

Double checked spacing based off of the actual box

realized SCS has pretty steep unit discount that doesn't allow for mirrored parts

made a few revisions, made mirrorable, improved strength

ordered

install went smoothly, everything fits!

posts.title: Reading List
posts.summary: Articles and resources I have found useful.
posts.bodyHtml:
Engineering:

- Keychron Github A collection of step files of actual consumer products

- Elon & Sandy: Design Philosophy Parallels | PART 1 A two part series where Sandy Munro goes through a collection of case studies on engineering efficiency and philosophy. Highly highly recommend.


Engineering Management:

- A rant on personal engineering projects Joe Barnard's rant on understanding the scope and failure modes of personal engineering projects.


Personal management:

- It Is Your Responsibility to Follow Up Excellent advice from Alexey Guzey on following up with people online.

posts.title: NPS Research
posts.summary: A summary of 2 summers interning at Naval Postgraduate school.
posts.heroBlurb: Some SLM Steel samples undergoing heat treatment.
posts.bodyHtml:
I spent two summers at NPS during high school. It was my first proper job outside of family work and I think it went pretty well! 

Actually a good lesson in getting jobs here too: 

I had been doing some 3D printing on my own for about a year at that point. At the open house for the internship program (students basically all walk around and hand out resumes/talk to profs) I talked to a few spots but made an alright impression with Dr. Luhrs. However the day after I then sent her and a few other spots an email talking about my interest in the project and why I think I'd be a good fit, citing my 3d printing experience. She later told me that that's the reason she hired me. It is your responsibility to follow up!

Regardless, the first internship was in person and was really fun. Basically just tensile testing of maraging steel parts but we used a lot of different hardware. Whenever I got to use the SEM I was stressed out because 1.5 million is a lot of money to not break for a kid haha. I helped heat treat samples, log and mount composite plastic samples in a UV machine, and mount metal ones in the salt fog machine. I also helped another professor design a jig for her graphene research and printed it on my home printer. I was ahead of schedule so I also designed some hardness testing samples for the titanium printer we had on site. That was really fun to watch/help with. Then polished the samples and tested their hardness along the edge of the part. 

paper is here: https://www.mdpi.com/2075-4701/10/2/218


My second internship was work from home during Covid. I was designing some phase change material fluid wicks and testing using my printer. I made a good number of iterations but it really was just not the same, way harder to be excited and motivated at home vs in a lab with other people haha. 

Overall though a great experience.

(V1 - 4/25/26)
